EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of elevated instability and warm air advection resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms early in the morning on April 22, 2022 across northwest Iowa into southwest Minnesota. One storm took on supercell characteristics and produced severe hail up to the size of golf balls across Osceola County.

Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours. With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind.

Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A briefly intensifying storm within a low-level jet produced an isolated wind gust at the Storm Lake Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KSLB southwest of Storm Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall trends of drier than normal conditions persisted for October over much of Iowa.  While there was some scattered rainfall, the overall amounts remained mostly below normal for the month.  Severe drought conditions remained in large portions of the state with some extreme conditions in northwest to north central Iowa.  These conditions likely impacted crop yields across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was common across much of Clarke County for the month of October as a deficit of rainfall continued along with above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Friday afternoon - early Saturday morning.  This snow impacted the evening commute causing hazardous travel, especially between Des Moines and Iowa City on Interstate 80 and along I-380 between Iowa City and Cedar Rapids.  The highest snow totals between 8 and 12 inches were common roughly along a line from Des Moines to Keokuk.  Snow amounts between 4-7 inches were found across much of eastern Iowa towards west central Illinois.  Lesser amounts of 1-3 inches were reported in northern and north central Illinois.  ||Illinois, Iowa, and Missouri DOT reported slick and snow covered roads through much of the event.  Winds between 20-30 mph were also seen causing some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter, COOP, and public reports of 6-10 inches of snow fell across portions of Jefferson County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 15th. The highest snowfall report from the event was 10 inches in Fairfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved south through the Missouri River Valley on January 14th. Snow on the northeast side of the storm fell across parts of northeast Iowa with amounts generally ranging from 5 to 8 inches across Mitchell, Floyd and Chickasaw Counties. The highest reported total was 8.7 inches in Nora Springs (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Chickasaw County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted much of southeast Iowa, western Illinois, and northeast Missouri. Widespread snowfall ranging between 5 and 10 inches of snow was reported in the hardest hit areas, with 1 to 4 inches surrounding and gusty winds to follow. This event started and ended as a snow event, as cold air was in place prior. With the gusty winds, blowing and drifting snow was observed, reducing visibilities and making it difficult to measure the snowfall. Just as this storm was moving out, very cold temperatures moved in, with wind chills between -15 to -30 felt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter and public reports of 4-7 inches of snow fell across portions of Benton County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 2nd. Verification was also considered using snow amounts and other combined hazards/impacts, with the highest snowfall report near Norway at 6.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Friday afternoon - early Saturday morning.  This snow impacted the evening commute causing hazardous travel, especially between Des Moines and Iowa City on Interstate 80 and along I-380 between Iowa City and Cedar Rapids.  The highest snow totals between 8 and 12 inches were common roughly along a line from Des Moines to Keokuk.  Snow amounts between 4-7 inches were found across much of eastern Iowa towards west central Illinois.  Lesser amounts of 1-3 inches were reported in northern and north central Illinois.  ||Illinois, Iowa, and Missouri DOT reported slick and snow covered roads through much of the event. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter, COOP, and public reports of 5-8 inches of snow fell across portions of Benton County. Blowing and drifting snow continued to cause problems on roads after plowing, lasting into January 15th. The highest snowfall report from the event was 6.8 inches in Vinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall trends of drier than normal conditions persisted for October over much of Iowa.  While there was some scattered rainfall, the overall amounts remained mostly below normal for the month.  Severe drought conditions remained in large portions of the state with some extreme conditions in northwest to north central Iowa.  These conditions likely impacted crop yields across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was common across much of Warren County for the month of October as a deficit of rainfall continued along with above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sporadic wind gusts to around 60 mph occurred in the strong cold advection behind a strengthening clipper system as it moved into the Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was measured near Maurice (site KSXK).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Iowa on January 24th, with morning highs in the 30s that tumbled through the day. By the early morning hours of January 25th, wind chills across northern Iowa were -30 degrees or colder. Temperatures recovered slightly during the daytime hours of the 25th before tumbling once again overnight into the morning of the 26th across northern and northeastern Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Kansas across Iowa initiating severe storms and tornadoes in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported roof damage and a flipped trampoline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of supercell thunderstorms moved through the region beginning on the evening of June 14 and continuing into the early morning hours of June 15. The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 75 to 115 mph, hail 1 to 3 inches in diameter, and two EF1 tornadoes. Minor flooding was also reported in Omaha. The storms caused widespread tree damage, and some structural damage to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported estimated wind gusts of 90 mph.

The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 75 to 115 mph, hail 1 to 3 inches in diameter, and two EF1 tornadoes. Minor flooding was also reported in Omaha. The storms caused widespread tree damage, and some structural damage to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported power lines down and power out in Glenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm embedded within widespread rain across eastern Iowa. produced penny sized hail in Lee County Iowa.

The overall trends of drier than normal conditions persisted for October over much of Iowa.  While there was some scattered rainfall, the overall amounts remained mostly below normal for the month.  Severe drought conditions remained in large portions of the state with some extreme conditions in northwest to north central Iowa.  These conditions likely impacted crop yields across much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was common across much of Jasper County for the month of October as a deficit of rainfall continued along with above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the state with gusty northwest winds behind it. While most of the winds remained between 45 to 55 mph, the Mason City airport did report a wind gust of 61 mph. Winds remained breezy through the afternoon before diminishing in the evening.

Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening of February 21. In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 2 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 30 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21.

Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with 1.5 inches near Spirit Lake and 1.0 inch at Lake Park, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 32 mph at Milford and 30 mph near Spirit |Lake, visibility was around one mile and occasionally one-half mile or less between 0730-1330CST February 22. In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of February 18th, a powerful cold front quickly moved through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. A narrow band of intense snow squalls accompanied this front, bringing a near instantaneous onset of brief winter storm conditions. Roads went from dry to snow covered in a matter of minutes, temperatures fell from around 35 to 28 in minutes, and visibility fell from over 6 miles to under 1/4 mile as the snow squall arrived. Snow accumulation generally was a 1/2 inch to 1 inch, falling in around 1 hour. Once the snow squall ended, dry and windy conditions continued the rest of the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rapid onset of intense winter storm conditions with snow, blowing snow, and snow covered roads reported near DeWitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic surface ridge moved southward behind the light snowfall and freezing rain on February 21-22. Northerly winds which gusted at times to 35 to 50 mph gradually diminished as temperatures dropped to zero to 10 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -29F at Sergeant Bluff (site KSUX).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of isentropic lift across the area resulted in a light wintry mix on the morning of January 14th thanks to a warm layer aloft. Later that day the primary forcing from an inverted trough approached the area, enhancing the the background thermodynamic lift. The heaviest snow occurred in the evening an overnight hours as snow rates neared one inch per hour. This resulted in a broad area of six to ten inches of snow across much of the state, with pockets of a foot or more in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low lifted out of the four corners region and into the central US, tracking across Missouri. A broad deformation zone on the north side of the system swept across the southern half of Iowa on January 1st, fed by moisture transport out of the Gulf and the Pacific Northwest. With ample moisture and deep saturation within the dendritic growth zone, snow to liquid ratios were rather high.  A large portion of central and southeast Iowa saw snow accumulations of four to six inches, with localized pockets of seven to nine inch accumulations. This was accompanied by gusty winds ushering in arctic air. As snow was still falling in southern Iowa, winds gusted to between 25 to 35 mph with periods of visibility below a quarter mile. As the system exited the area, the arctic air mass settled in, and wind chills across northern Iowa dropped into the -20 to -30 degree range overnight into the morning of January 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped very briefly as low as -36F at site KSXK near Maurice, with -32F at mesonet site RTNI4 and -30F at personal weather station FW6032 in Hawarden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low lifted out of the four corners region and into the central US, tracking across Missouri. A broad deformation zone on the north side of the system swept across the southern half of Iowa on January 1st, fed by moisture transport out of the Gulf and the Pacific Northwest. With ample moisture and deep saturation within the dendritic growth zone, snow to liquid ratios were rather high.  A large portion of central and southeast Iowa saw snow accumulations of four to six inches, with localized pockets of seven to nine inch accumulations. This was accompanied by gusty winds ushering in arctic air. As snow was still falling in southern Iowa, winds gusted to between 25 to 35 mph with periods of visibility below a quarter mile. As the system exited the area, the arctic air mass settled in, and wind chills across northern Iowa dropped into the -20 to -30 degree range overnight into the morning of January 2nd.

Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening of February 21. In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 2 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 30 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21. Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with 0.6 inch at Sibley, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 43 mph at Sheldon and 40 mph at Sioux Falls, visibility was around one mile or less at times between 0730-1200CST February 22. In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F.

A broad area of isentropic lift across the area resulted in a light wintry mix on the morning of January 14th thanks to a warm layer aloft. Later that day the primary forcing from an inverted trough approached the area, enhancing the the background thermodynamic lift. The heaviest snow occurred in the evening an overnight hours as snow rates neared one inch per hour. This resulted in a broad area of six to ten inches of snow across much of the state, with pockets of a foot or more in some locations.

With cold air entrenched across the area, a mid-level wave dug southward through the Plains. A swath of snowfall developed in the leading warm advection zone northeast of the track of low pressure from South Dakota to Missouri. The highest snowfall of 5 to 11 inches was northeast of a line from De Smet, SD to Ida Grove, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 0800-2000CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 8.9 inches at Sioux Rapids and 6.0 inches at Storm Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly moderate to heavy at times between 1000-1500CST, and produced dangerous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Rock Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -37F at mesonet site DONI4 3 miles east-northeast of Doon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass with temperatures in the teens below zero spread across northeast Iowa toward the end of January. These temperatures combined with wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills around 35 below during the morning of January 25th. Another round of dangerously cold wind chills occurred from the late evening of the 25th into the morning of the 26th. Wind chills during this period dropped into the -35 to -40 range. The coldest reported wind chills were 40 below in Charles City (Floyd County) and by a mesonet station near Saratoga (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills around 35 below occurred across Chickasaw County during the morning of January 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -33F at KSUX near Sergeant Bluff and -32F at mesonet site RSCI4 near Moville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -33F at KSLB near Storm Lake and and -32F at mesonet site RSCI4 near Moville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass with temperatures in the teens below zero spread across northeast Iowa toward the end of January. These temperatures combined with wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills around 35 below during the morning of January 25th. Another round of dangerously cold wind chills occurred from the late evening of the 25th into the morning of the 26th. Wind chills during this period dropped into the -35 to -40 range. The coldest reported wind chills were 40 below in Charles City (Floyd County) and by a mesonet station near Saratoga (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills around 35 below occurred across Floyd County during the morning of January 25th. The coldest wind chill of 37 below was reported by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of isentropic lift across the area resulted in a light wintry mix on the morning of January 14th thanks to a warm layer aloft. Later that day the primary forcing from an inverted trough approached the area, enhancing the the background thermodynamic lift. The heaviest snow occurred in the evening an overnight hours as snow rates neared one inch per hour. This resulted in a broad area of six to ten inches of snow across much of the state, with pockets of a foot or more in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across the area on Friday March 25th, with strong gusty winds developing behind the front. There were widespread wind speeds of 20 to 30 MPH with gusts up to 45 MPH. Rain showers developed behind the cold front with reported wind gusts in these showers of 50 to 60 MPH during the afternoon and evening hours. There was a wind gust of 67 mph at the Cedar Rapids Airport. Winds began to diminish after 10 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport recorded a wind gust of 67 mph, which was brief and occurred with a nearby rain shower. The FAA tower reported a gust of 54 knots measured by other equipment at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening midlevel trough over the central Rockies advected north an unstable airmass into Nebraska and Iowa. Storms initiated to the northeast of the triple point leading to two tornadoes and numerous hail and strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported that the housing for a traffic light at the intersection of Division and 76th Streets were physically turned by strong thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reporter reported a large tree was uprooted. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed about 2.5 miles southwest of Vinton Saturday evening, and tracked northeast through the northwest portion of Vinton, before lifting northeast of town. The tornado was on the ground for about 16 minutes, along a 14 mile path. The maximum tornado width was about 400 yards. The tornado was rated a strong EF-1, with maximum estimated winds around 110 mph. Southwest of Vinton, the tornado snapped numerous power poles and damaged trees and outbuildings. In northwest Vinton, the tornado damaged numerous structures, including mobile homes, houses, and wood frame metal buildings in an industrial park. Numerous residents of the mobile home park that was impacted went to the designated storm shelter built by the city of Vinton. Outdoor warning sirens were activated 3 times before the tornado moved into far northwest Vinton. There were no reported injuries or fatalities. The tornado continued northeast through rural areas, causing tree and outbuilding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado produced intermittent damage along a roughly 4 mile path. The peak estimated winds were 90 to 95 MPH with a maximum width of 300 yards. The most significant damage was noted at a farmstead on 160th Street, east of 100th Avenue, where several outbuildings were damage or destroyed, and trees were damaged or snapped. Boards were also impaled into the roof and walls of a barn.  Additional damage was noted at a farmstead on Utah Avenue north of Duck Creek. Several outbuildings were damaged with some losing roof panels and damaging other structures. Tree damage was noted with several trees uprooted or snapped. A wood plank was driven into the ground. Primarily intermittent tree damage was noted along the remainder of the track, with large branches down or trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area late on the evening of April 21 into April 22. While thunderstorms remained below severe criteria, deep saturation and precipitable water values over 1.5 inches allowed for a band of heavier precipitation to set up over a narrow corridor in central Iowa. Several locations received over 3 inches of rain and a few areas saw flash flooding within towns, resulting in brief road closures. Rain ended by 11 am across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 3.90 inches of rain.

Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed south of Highway 9 and west of Warbler Avenue and moved northeast.  The tornado encountered a farmstead on the south side of Highway 9 producing EF1 damage to trees and an outbuilding.  The tornado continued northeast and remained in rural farmland the rest of its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area late on the evening of April 21 into April 22. While thunderstorms remained below severe criteria, deep saturation and precipitable water values over 1.5 inches allowed for a band of heavier precipitation to set up over a narrow corridor in central Iowa. Several locations received over 3 inches of rain and a few areas saw flash flooding within towns, resulting in brief road closures. Rain ended by 11 am across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flash flooding relayed through trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed across all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri on the afternoon of Thursday May 26, 2022. These showers and storms formed in response to an area of low pressure moving across eastern Missouri into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening. This activity slowly rotated around the low, allowing showers and storms to produce torrential rainfall over areas they impacted. During the evening, repeated rounds of showers and storms impacted the city of Danville, IA, and led to flash flooding across the city. Per emergency management, water flowed into homes, apartments, and a school, and washed out several gravel roads on the west side of town. There was an unofficial measured rainfall amount of 9 inches in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time of the event is estimated based on radar. Emergency Manager reported several gravel roads washed out in the Scotch Grove and Central Park areas. Shoulders on roads were also washed out with roads being closed. Additionally, water from Mineral Creek was over the roads.  A personal weather station in Scotch Grove reported 2.0 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mercy Hospital ambulance reported penny to nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado continued into Boone County from Polk County.  This tornado was observed via a TDS on the WSR-88D radar and it remained in rural cropland for the duration of its path in Boone county before lifting. No damage was observed to any damage indicators via the ground survey, hence the EFU rating.

It did some damage to the shingles and facia along with minor tree damage, all rated as EF0 damage.  The tornado continued to the northeast across a mixture of farmland and tree rows producing mostly minor tree damage before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area late on the evening of April 21 into April 22. While thunderstorms remained below severe criteria, deep saturation and precipitable water values over 1.5 inches allowed for a band of heavier precipitation to set up over a narrow corridor in central Iowa. Several locations received over 3 inches of rain and a few areas saw flash flooding within towns, resulting in brief road closures. Rain ended by 11 am across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 2.98 inches of rain. Light rain continues.

Dry air allowed for deep mixing and gusty winds aloft to be mixed down. Winds were breezy much f the day, with many sites experiencing winds sustained at 40 mph and a few areas gusting over 60 mph.

Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed just west of Iowa Avenue south of 120th Street west of Kanawha and immediately produced minor damage to outbuildings.  The tornado continued east northeast and grazed the northwest edge of Kanawha with tree branches downed along with minor damage to a few houses, mainly from missing shingles.  The tornado dissipated not far northeast of the town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This QLCS tornado developed to the southwest of the intersection of 43rd Street SW and South Federal Avenue.  The tornado crossed Federal Avenue and produced EF1 damage to several commercial buildings on the east side of the road.   The tornado continued northeast and damaged another commercial building along 245th Street near the Union Pacific railroad.  Additional damage occurred to trees along S Kentucky Avenue and eventually near 4th Street SE near Yorktown Pike.   The tornado lifted before encountering Buena Vista University - Mason City Campus.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado had an unusual path with a fishhook shaped track.  The tornado started to the northwest just south of 230th Street between Elm and Florida Avenues.  The tornado produced EF1 damage at a farmstead on the south side of 230th street, mostly to trees. The tornado began to loop northward along Elm Avenue producing damage at a couple of farms.  The tornado continued to curve toward the northeast and eventually an east northeast heading ending a little over a mile southwest of Rutland, IA.

This occurred just north of 180th Street west of Georgia Avenue.  The tornado had more of a north heading upon crossing 170th Street and impacted a couple of farmstead, producing EF1 damage.   The tornado continued to curve counter-clockwise eventually reaching a northeast heading. Upon crossing Iowa Avenue just north of 145th Street, numerous sizeable power poles were snapped with EF2 damage occurring.  The tornado diminished about one and a quarter mile southeast of Bode, IA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of fast moving storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of May 30th. These storms produced wind gusts between 60 and 70 mph that blew down trees in Rudd (Floyd County). A spotter measured a 72 mph gust near Rockford (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Rudd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Sioux City was around 25 percent of normal, with temperatures from 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) at the start of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile mesonet station measured a 62 mph wind gust. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Kansas across Iowa initiating severe storms and tornadoes in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple storm chasers reported a tornado northeast of Emerson that produced minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of dime and quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the approach of a strong mid-level wave, a cluster of multicellular storms evolved within a weakly unstable and weakly-sheared environment.  A fair amount of dry air at lower levels made for 600 J/kg or more of DCAPE, with storms producing some spotty significant wind damage along a track from near Paullina to around Primghar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm outbuilding was destroyed by thunderstorm winds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size. By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly above-normal temperatures along with below-normal precipitation led to stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions remained in place through July, which featured temperatures of 1 to 2 degrees above normal with 75 to 90 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to worsening drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed late-month, with temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly above-normal temperatures along with below-normal precipitation led to stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought during the first week of July, which featured temperatures of 1 to 2 degrees above normal and near to slightly below normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven dime size hail caused some damage to vegetation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSCI4 1 mile southwest of Moville. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF0 tornado developed in Floyd County and moved into Mitchell County southwest of Osage. The tornado moved to the northeast across rural parts of southern Mitchell County before dissipating south of Osage. Damage was primarily limited to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs down via broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reports trees down in Kanawha. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa was placed within the warm sector of a strong surface low pressure system, and the tight pressure gradient and deep mixing allowed for gusty south winds. A handful of station even gusted over 50 kts with some damage reported. Meanwhile, a dryline initiated convection across parts of northern Iowa in the late afternoon and evening. These storm primarily produced strong winds, though quarter sized hail was reported by one observer. Later that night, the cold front brought through another round of non-severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cattle barns a complete loss. Delayed report, time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph was measured by personal weather station KIAROCKR18 6 miles east of Rock Rapids. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight thunderstorms across Nebraska moved into Iowa in the early morning hours of May 8th. The nocturnal storms we fueled in part by the low level jet, and continued to be sustained by steady moisture transport across the area. As storms moved across the Des Moines metro, a lightning strike is believed to have started a fire which caused significant damage to several condos. By afternoon some strengthening occurred and while thunderstorms remained below severe limits, penny sized hail was reported with one storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of a nickel next to a slightly smaller hail stone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning and afternoon convection moved across parts of southern Minnesota along a warm front. Later in the afternoon, convection developed in South Dakota and moved into Northern Iowa and southern Minnesota as the upper level shortwave trough axis passed over. Remnant outflow boundaries from the morning convection helped to steer the convection, which strengthened into a well organized mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV). While the primary influences were over Minnesota, there was wind damage reported in northern Iowa with the trailing end of the convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of multiple trees and branches broken off. Largest 1.5 ft in diameter.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KSLB 3 miles west-southwest of Lakeside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size. By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large farm outbuilding had part of the roof and walls blown off east of Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.

Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small outbuilding and large tree knocked over from the inflow into the storm at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey found that a brief weak tornado damaged a shed and some trees. The peak estimated wind speed is 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was near zero due to strong winds and dust for around 10 minutes. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 79 mph was measured by personal weather station KIAHARTL8 5 miles west of Hartley. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Rock Valley and Sioux Center was less than 25 percent of normal, with temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly average month for precipitation and temperature led to only spotty improvement in drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted through May with cooler temperatures and periodic  precipitation which totaled around 75 percent of normal, balanced by warmer than normal temperatures and drier conditions at the end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa was placed within the warm sector of a strong surface low pressure system, and the tight pressure gradient and deep mixing allowed for gusty south winds. A handful of station even gusted over 50 kts with some damage reported. Meanwhile, a dryline initiated convection across parts of northern Iowa in the late afternoon and evening. These storm primarily produced strong winds, though quarter sized hail was reported by one observer. Later that night, the cold front brought through another round of non-severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branch down over the roadway. Delayed report, time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust from AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down just outside of Ridgeway and moved into the very northwest side of town before dissipating. A few farm outbuildings were damaged along the path, and a livestock trailer was flipped onto its top.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 5, 2022 a total of 15 tornadoes occurred across the state of Iowa as a warm front lifted across the area. Supercells initially developed across southwest Iowa in the vicinity of the triple point of the surface low with strong, rotating updrafts. Longevity was initially lacking with the storms, producing funnel clouds, brief tornadoes, and hail in southwest Iowa. As the cells moved northeast and matured, larger hail began to fall with golf ball sized hail falling in Adams county and baseball sized hail into Adair county. Following this period of large hail the storms produced a tornado which would remain on the ground for just over 70 miles, carving a path across central Iowa from southwest of Winterset to just northeast of Newton. The tornado peaked in Winterset, Iowa, producing EF-4 damage with wind speeds estimated near 170 mph. The tornado continued across the southern portion of the Des Moines metro area and to Newton. This is the furthest north EF-4 tornado in the CONUS this early in the year on record, the first EF-4 tornado in Iowa since October 4, 2013 which occurred in Woodbury and Cherokee Counties, and is the second longest tornado in Iowa since 1980, behind the longest occurring on June 7, 1984 at a length of 117 miles across southern Iowa. As the storm skimmed the southern Des Moines metro, cells across south central became tornadic, producing several destructive tornadoes including an EF-3 tornado in Chariton, Iowa. This heartbreaking day resulted in seven fatalities, six in Winterset and one in Chariton, making it the deadliest tornado outbreak in Iowa since 2008. At 15 tornadoes, this is also one of the earliest outbreaks of this magnitude to occur so early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of a funnel cloud 6 miles west southwest of Macksburg, IA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed across the area with strong winds developing behind it. Deep mixing allowed for widespread gusts of 40-50 mph with a few locations briefly gusting to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mason City ASOS reported a 58 mph gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large maple tree uprooted and fell onto roof of home. Time of occurrence unknown so time of report used.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave rough moved across the area, producing weak showers and thunderstorms and breezy winds. The thunderstorms remained sub-severe and the accompanying environmental winds were generally under 40 mph, however a few stronger gusts did occur with light showers including a single gust over 58 mph at Boone, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Boone AWOS reported a 61 mph gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado which started in Madison county and passed into Warren county.  The tornado remained very broad with a maximum width of up to 500 yards at times.  Video evidence supports a multi-vortex circulation with the tornado across much of Warren county.  The tornado cross Interstate 35 shortly after entering the county but fortunately, no vehicles were impacted.  The tornado remained in rural areas to the west southwest of Norwalk producing mostly tree and outbuilding damage.  The tornado passed along the southeast edge of Norwalk producing EF0 to EF1 damage   along Coolidge Street and along Highway G14 near Warrior Run Golf Course.  The tornado crossed Highway 5 just west of the intersections of Highway 5 and Highway 69 approaching the southeast side of Des Moines.  The tornado entered into Polk county shortly after crossing Highway 69 near the intersection of Highway 69 and Indianola Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado passed from Warren county into Polk county near the intersection of Highway 69 and County Line Road.   The tornado crossed E Pine Avenue removing the roof of a house and then clipped the north edge of Avon producing EF2 damage to a couple of houses. The damage continued to the northeast into less inhabited areas of eastern Polk county, however the tornado remained strong with EF2 damage at several locations along its path.  The tornado crossed University Avenue near its intersection with NE 112th Street and then exited the county into Jasper county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved into Jasper county from Polk county.  The tornado remained largely in rural areas for much of its path across Jasper county.  Despite its rural venture, the tornado impacted several residences in western Jasper county producing several areas of EF2 damage.  The tornado did pass not far from the southeast side of Colfax damaging structures near the intersection of Highway 117 and S 44th Avenue West.  The tornado crossed Interstate 80 east of Colfax near mile marker 160 and then proceeded towards Newton, grazing both the communities of Lambs Grove and Newton on the north sides.  Most of the damage was to trees and minor damage to structures including loss of shingles and broken windows.  The tornado did remove about a 100 foot segment of the roof of the former TPI Composites Plant northeast of Newton with the tornado dissipating shortly thereafter.  In all, the tornado had a path length of just over 70 miles and passed through four counties including Madison, Warren, Polk and Jasper. The six deaths were the most by a tornado in Iowa since the Parkersburg tornado in May of 2008.  The damage was into the hundreds of millions of dollars.

The torndo remained in rural areas of the county for the duration of its path.  Damage was primarily to broken power poles, although residential house damage was also noted.  A roof was removed from a house just east of Garden Grove producing EF2 damage with other damage to nearby outbuildings also noted.  The tornado exited into Wayne county just north of 140th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a round of heavy snow to a small portion of northeast Iowa from the evening of March 6th into the morning of the 7th. Snowfall amounts around 6 inches were common across Clayton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 6 inches of snow fell across Clayton County.

However, the bulk of the path interacted with either empty cropland or woodland areas with numerous trees being toppled. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 5, 2022 a total of 15 tornadoes occurred across the state of Iowa as a warm front lifted across the area. Longevity was initially lacking with the storms, producing funnel clouds, brief tornadoes, and hail in southwest Iowa. As the cells moved northeast and matured, larger hail began to fall with golf ball sized hail falling in Adams county and baseball sized hail into Adair county. Following this period of large hail the storms produced a tornado which would remain on the ground for just over 70 miles, carving a path across central Iowa from southwest of Winterset to just northeast of Newton. The tornado peaked in Winterset, Iowa, producing EF-4 damage with wind speeds estimated near 170 mph. The tornado continued across the southern portion of the Des Moines metro area and to Newton. This is the furthest north EF-4 tornado in the CONUS this early in the year on record, the first EF-4 tornado in Iowa since October 4, 2013 which occurred in Woodbury and Cherokee Counties, and is the second longest tornado in Iowa since 1980, behind the longest occurring on June 7, 1984 at a length of 117 miles across southern Iowa. As the storm skimmed the southern Des Moines metro, cells across south central became tornadic, producing several destructive tornadoes including an EF-3 tornado in Chariton, Iowa. This heartbreaking day resulted in seven fatalities, six in Winterset and one in Chariton, making it the deadliest tornado outbreak in Iowa since 2008. At 15 tornadoes, this is also one of the earliest outbreaks of this magnitude to occur so early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed as two other tornadoes dissipated in southern Lucas county.  The tornado moved to the northeast across rural areas of southern into central Lucas county primarily in wooded areas and cropland with little damage.  Of note however, was a satellite tornado observed in chasing video (in seperate Storm Data entry) which developed north of the main tornado and moved south toward the main circulation, doing a complete revolution around the main tornado before being absorbed.  Shortly afterwards, the tornado crossed State Highway 14 south of Chariton destroying a conservation center which was rated EF3.  The tornado continued to the northeast removing a roof from a house along 235th Trail  and passed through an open field before entering Red Haw State Park.  The tornado ripped through a campground in the park with one fatality and one injury occurring.  The tornado exited the park and crossed Highway 34 east of Chariton with numerous outbuildings and power poles damage or destroyed.  The tornado then remained in rural wooded areas and cropland for the rest of its path with little additional damage noted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the first segment of this devastating tornado which passed through four counties including Madison, Warren, Polk and Jasper.  The tornado began northwest of Macksburg in western Madision county.  The tornado remained in mostly rural cropland and wooded areas of western Madison county.  The tornado passed through Pammel State Park southwest of Winterset producing extensive tree damage as the tornado continued to widen into a wedge tornado.  Upon exiting the park, the tornado travelled along Carver Road.  Several residential homes were extensively damaged or destroyed along Carver Road and six deaths occurred along this stretch of road.  The tornado was at peak intensity at this time with EF4 damage occurring west of the intersection of Carver Road and Highway 169 with a house completely destoyed and removed from the foundation.  After departing the Carver Road corridor, the tornado progressed just southeast of Winterset and eventually passed east of town and crossing State Highway 92.  Damage in these areas was primarily to trees and outbuildings although a few homes were also impacted with mostly EF1 to EF2 damage.  From Highway 92 to where the tornado exited the county, it remained mostly in rural cropland and wooded areas with numerous trees impacted.  The tornado then moved into Warren county from Madison county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed southwest of Allerton and moved northeast snapping power poles along Idaho Road before clipping the southeast edge of Allerton.  A manufactured house had its roof removed on the edge of town with the tornado snapping power poles and a back stop at a park on the east side of town.  The tornado then remained in rural areas of central Wayne county with little damage noted.  The circulation stayed just southeast of Corydon but did destroy a machine shed along Highway 2 east of town.  The tornado continued northeast before dissipating north of Newton Road west of 180th Street.

This tornado began just north of 150th street west of P13 and moved east northeast.  It destroyed a small outbuilding shortly after developing and then remained mostly in rural cropland.  It did produce some minor damage to a farmstead on the county line road before entering Union county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a satellite tornado which formed near the tornado which went on to impact areas just south and east of Chariton, Iowa.  This satellite tornado was captured on video by storm chasers and left a path observed in high resolution satellite imagery.  This tornado developed north of the main tornado and travelled due west before turning ninety degrees to the south with movement toward the main circulation.  The tornado did a full revolution around the main tornado before being absorbed into the overall circulation.  There was some tree damage associated with the satellite tornado but otherwise did not impact any other structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed from the same supercell that produced the Winterset to Newton tornado.  It remained in rural areas of central and eastern Jasper county downing power poles and trees along its path.  Machine sheds were destroyed at a residence along East 116th Street North with additional damage to outbuildings along East 132nd Street North.  The tornado passed just west and north of the hamlet of Newburg before exiting the county into Poweshiek county just south of North 99th Avenue East.

This is the furthest north EF-4 tornado in the CONUS this early in the year on record, the first EF-4 tornado in Iowa since October 4, 2013 which occurred in Woodbury and Cherokee Counties, and is the second longest tornado in Iowa since 1980, behind the longest occurring on June 7, 1984 at a length of 117 miles across southern Iowa. As the storm skimmed the southern Des Moines metro, cells across south central became tornadic, producing several destructive tornadoes including an EF-3 tornado in Chariton, Iowa. This heartbreaking day resulted in seven fatalities, six in Winterset and one in Chariton, making it the deadliest tornado outbreak in Iowa since 2008. At 15 tornadoes, this is also one of the earliest outbreaks of this magnitude to occur so early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough lifted out of the central plains and into Iowa in the afternoon. Scattered convection on the leading edge of the trough produced a couple damaging wind gusts and hail thanks to moderate instability around 1500 J/kg and dry low levels. As the column saturated the instability diminished and reduced the threat for severe storms but increasing the rainfall efficiency as the system congealed into a mesoscale convective system overnight. A widespread two to three inches of rain fell across the area. No flash flooding was reported and while appreciated, the rain did little to reduce the expanding drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thermal ridge continued to keep much of the central CONUS hot in early August, and on the evening of August 6th a front sunk south into northern Iowa. This provided the impetus for convective development that for the most part remained sub severe other than a gust in Kossuth County. After the initial cluster of thunderstorms passed, additional storms developed along the front and trained across northern Iowa overnight. This resulted in a pocket of heavy rain with over six inches reported in a few locations. Despite the heavy rain, no flash flooding was reported, due at least in part to ongoing drought in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust from personal weather station KIALUVER4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary stretched across Iowa and into Nebraska in the early morning hours of August 29th with the mid level 850 mb boundary lagging behind and better associated with a ribbon of high instability with MUCAPE over 3000 J/kg. A storm developed along the boundary in western Iowa and persisted across much of southern Iowa through the morning, producing intermittent large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter sized hail with no accompanying strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold core low moved over the area, dragging along a weak and slow moving surface low. Although weak, sufficient low level and upper forcing aided in developing strong to severe storms. Instability and shear were strong enough for a few supercell thunderstorms to develop and bring hail, strong winds, and heavy rain to the area. The storms were slow moving. which allowed some storms to anchor over locations for an extended period of time, resulting in flash flooding. Some repeated storms over the same area also led to similar results. Severe weather reports were mostly in the form of hail, as these storms produced hail very efficiently. The largest hail reported was 1.5, and there were some isolated reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that several yards and alleyways on the south side of Conesville had water up to a foot deep in them.
