EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Winter Storm Warning was issued for Dec 21 until the 23rd. Initially, peak snowfall amounts were forecast to range from 3 to 7 inches. As the event neared, the final forecast called for 2 to 3 inches across the area. The WSW was maintained as 40 to 50 mph wind gusts were expected to lead to near blizzard conditions. A brief period of freezing drizzle was also forecast and was observed.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2.5 inches with a peak of 2.5 inches observed in Fremont County. Snowfall amounts were difficult to measure accuately. Blizzard like conditions largely weren't realized. Some ground stations in southwest Iowa did observe visibility less than or equal to a mile for 2 to 3 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported 1.5 inches of snowfall at Clarinda. The AWOS at Clarinda observed visibility less than or equal to 1 mile for 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds.||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches were downed by thunderstorm winds, with one approximately 8 inch pine tree split.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few moderate-sized tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

Instability and shear were strong enough for a few supercell thunderstorms to develop and bring hail, strong winds, and heavy rain to the area. The storms were slow moving. which allowed some storms to anchor over locations for an extended period of time, resulting in flash flooding. Some repeated storms over the same area also led to similar results. Severe weather reports were mostly in the form of hail, as these storms produced hail very efficiently. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 4.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary moving across the state allowed for a cluster of thunderstorms to develop north of the Minnesota Iowa border in the morning and continue south southeast into Iowa through the afternoon. The overall convective parameter was not the most conducive to severe criteria thanks mostly to fairly weak forcing, however as the storms approached Zearing, Iowa the ingredients came together just right. The storm quickly strengthened, resulting in golf ball sized wind driven hail. This caused significant roof, siding, and tree damage in a swath through town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail ongoing at time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a hot day with temperatures nearing 100 degrees at many locations, a front passed across the area overnight and brought scattered convection to the area through the morning hours of August 3rd. While most of this activity did remain sub severe, a thunderstorm wind gust of 66 mph was recorded in Sac County associated with outflow from a thunderstorm passing to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ERYI4 Early Iowa RWIS. Outflow from thunderstorm to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 80 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold core low moved over the area, dragging along a weak and slow moving surface low. Although weak, sufficient low level and upper forcing aided in developing strong to severe storms. Instability and shear were strong enough for a few supercell thunderstorms to develop and bring hail, strong winds, and heavy rain to the area. The storms were slow moving. which allowed some storms to anchor over locations for an extended period of time, resulting in flash flooding. Some repeated storms over the same area also led to similar results. Severe weather reports were mostly in the form of hail, as these storms produced hail very efficiently. The largest hail reported was 1.5, and there were some isolated reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that hail has been falling for the past 10 minutes and that most of the stones were pea to nickel size.

This resulted in a pocket of heavy rain with over six inches reported in a few locations. Despite the heavy rain, no flash flooding was reported, due at least in part to ongoing drought in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 5.36 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low sinking south into the area allowed for scattered storms to develop across central Iowa in the afternoon. Steep lapse rates and deep layer shear allowed for the development of organized updrafts while low melting levels supported larger hail development. Several storms strengthened and prompted warnings, though few resulted in any severe reports. The strongest quickly developed over the Des Moines metro, where the time between a clear radar image and golf ball sized hail was just over 20 minutes. Hail pummeled the west side of the metro for nearly 30 minutes, resulting in hundreds of vehicles with significant damage. One high school in West Des Moines also reported damage to the building which resulted in water leaking into the library and a rush to protect the books. After the hail ended the storms continued to train across the metro, resulting in flash flooding a a couple of water rescues due to stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed large branches and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of elevated instability and warm air advection resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms early in the morning on April 22, 2022 across northwest Iowa into southwest Minnesota. One storm took on supercell characteristics and produced severe hail up to the size of golf balls across Osceola County.
Modest instability developed ahead of a cool front that moved across the area on the 29th.  This allowed an isolated thunderstorm to develop and produce 1 inch hail in Muscatine County.

A surface low sinking south into the area allowed for scattered storms to develop across central Iowa in the afternoon. Steep lapse rates and deep layer shear allowed for the development of organized updrafts while low melting levels supported larger hail development. Several storms strengthened and prompted warnings, though few resulted in any severe reports. The strongest quickly developed over the Des Moines metro, where the time between a clear radar image and golf ball sized hail was just over 20 minutes. Hail pummeled the west side of the metro for nearly 30 minutes, resulting in hundreds of vehicles with significant damage. One high school in West Des Moines also reported damage to the building which resulted in water leaking into the library and a rush to protect the books. After the hail ended the storms continued to train across the metro, resulting in flash flooding a a couple of water rescues due to stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime up to golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half-dollar size hail fell for a few minutes.

Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 69 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KSHL 3 miles north-northeast of Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust downed numerous 2 to 3 inch tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 6 to 8 inch diameter branches were downed by thunderstorm winds, as well as a  15 inch limb which blocked a city street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds.||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

A surface low sinking south into the area allowed for scattered storms to develop across central Iowa in the afternoon. Steep lapse rates and deep layer shear allowed for the development of organized updrafts while low melting levels supported larger hail development. Several storms strengthened and prompted warnings, though few resulted in any severe reports. The strongest quickly developed over the Des Moines metro, where the time between a clear radar image and golf ball sized hail was just over 20 minutes. Hail pummeled the west side of the metro for nearly 30 minutes, resulting in hundreds of vehicles with significant damage. One high school in West Des Moines also reported damage to the building which resulted in water leaking into the library and a rush to protect the books. After the hail ended the storms continued to train across the metro, resulting in flash flooding a a couple of water rescues due to stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WOI-5 reported three inch hail at the station in West Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14. Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with a few overturned semi-trucks across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently between 40 and 50 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KSXK near Maurice, with several peak gusts of 58 mph measured at 1155CST, 1335CST and 1435CST.  A one foot diameter tree was snapped around 1200CST in Hull, and an awning torn off a house in Orange City.

Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times during the afternoon at a personal weather station 2 miles west of Terril, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at 1244CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low passed across the area and while most thunderstorms activity pushed east of the area, a few showers lingered across Iowa. The environment was not primed for severe storms with modest instability, however there was sufficient 0-1 km CAPE that allowed for low level stretching, enhanced by the surface low. This resulting in several funnel clouds being reported across east central Iowa in the morning to early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.

The cold core low that moved into the area on 19th lingered into the 20th.  Sufficient instability and shear were present for the formation of many mini supercells this led to many funnel cloud reports with a few brief tornado touch downs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager relayed a report of a funnel cloud that was very close to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak warm air advection into the state allowed for convection the percolate much of the day, but by late afternoon a shortwave made its way into the area and provided the kinematic enhancement needed to strengthen the convection. Still with moderate CAPE and weak shear, there was little to support widespread coverage of strong storms. Only one storm reached severe limits but it dis result in large hail, tree damage from wind gusts, a brief funnel cloud, and a pocket of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported golf ball sized hail roughly half a mile west of Pocahontas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at Automated Weather Observing System site KLRJ near Le Mars, with a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 1255CST.

The continued heat put a damper on most severe development, however high DCAPE and strong cold pools did allow for gusty winds and subsequent tree and power line damage in Des Moines, likely the result of a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and Law enforcement tweets about multiple trees down, including pulling down power lines, mainly on the east side of the Des Moines metro. Time estimated via radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grassfire erupted 3 miles southeast of Merrill near the intersection of Key Avenue and 250th Street, and spread rapidly to the northeast.  The wind-driven fire scorched more than 1000 acres, destroying one home on the southwest corner of the intersection of County Road C44 and Lake Avenue.  At one point, seven area fire departments were on the fire.  This was the most significant of five concurrent fires across the county.  Visibility was near zero at times with smoke and blowing dust, and this resulted in one firefighting vehicle getting stuck in a ditch, overtaken and burned by the rapidly moving fire.

Strong winds downed power lines on a farm near Rock Rapids, which started a structure fire.  Numerous grass fires were also scattered around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds.||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long period of high non-thunderstorm winds produced a pair of wind gusts to 63 mph at the Sioux Gateway Regional Airport (KSUX) which occurred at 1922CST and 2031CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low and its attendant cold front moved across the area on the morning of August 3rd. A line of storms developed ahead of the front as it moved across eastern Iowa. Several of these storms become severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm winds of 55 to 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved across Iowa in early June, this one resulting in severe storms across southwest Iowa. Modest CAPE around 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear that helped to organize storms and support rotating updrafts capable of large hail and damaging winds. By evening storms across southwest Iowa were producing up to golf ball sized hail and winds damaged several trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind driven hail event that features hail up to half dollar sized and winds over 60 mph that lasted for 15 to 20 minutes. Just emerged corn and beans were pulverized, pine trees stripped of needles, and hardwood trees defoliated. Worst hail this spotter has seen in 50 years at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado developed on the south side of Doon, and traveled northeast across the southeast side of town before it dissipated at the eastern edge of town. Damage was primarily to trees, with some peripheral damage to a playset near the touchdown, and an older garage near the end of the track. The estimated maximum wind was 85 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated stronger storm within a line of thunderstorms pushing northeast across northwest Iowa produced sub-severe size hail.
A small area of around 2 inches of rainfall occurred near the Iowa Great Lakes on June 4-5. Runoff quickly brought a small portion of the upper Little Sioux River into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall pushed the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) above minor flood stage early on June 5, with a crest at 0.85 feet above flood stage late on June 5. The river fell back below flood stage on June 9. A minor amount of agricultural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough dropped out of the Canadian Rockies and across the upper midwest. Storms developed along a surface boundary in the afternoon, resulting in a few hail reports and a strong wind gust. In addition to the moderate CAPE , the backed wind profile supported some rotation within storms. While no tornadoes occurred there was a funnel cloud reported north of the Des Moines metro.  Later that night the deep warm cloud layer and high precipitable water values made for efficient rain producers. As storms continued overnight along a stationary boundary locally heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped numerous power poles, and downed a 3 foot diameter tree as well as many 2 to 4 inch diameter branches. Power was knocked out for several hours when falling tree limbs took down powerlines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant portion of the roof of a livestock building was removed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arc of thunderstorms developed along the nose of the low-level jet across northwest Iowa during the very early hours of June 25.  The thunderstorms became nearly stationary for a couple hours, and produced a very narrow band of heavy rainfall with spotty flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.1 inches in a little over an hour resulted in water flowing over 440th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures have led to the expansion of severe drought conditions in portions of northwest and north central Iowa by late June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Pocahontas county began to experience severe drought conditions by late June as below normal rainfall persisted in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters responded to a home struck by lightning, resulting in a fire in the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The relatively dry conditions of the past few months have led to continued severe drought conditions in portions of northwest and north central Iowa during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The continued lack of rainfall allowed severe drought conditions to persist over portions of Palo Alto county for all of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a brief tornado crossing Interstate 29 between Sergeant Bluff and Port Neal. The tornado caused no damage from which the NWS could assign an EF-scale rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted or snapped several trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following storms the previous day, the Des Moines River at Fleur Drive briefly went into flood stage on the 7th of June. It went into flood stage, 12.0 feet, at 5am CST on the 7th, peaked at 12.47 feet at 11:30pm CST on the 7th, and receded below flood stage at 1:30pm CST on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following storms the previous day, the Des Moines River at Fleur Drive briefly went into flood stage on the 7th of June. It went into flood stage, 12.0 feet, at 5am CST on the 7th, peaked at 12.47 feet at 11:30pm CST on the 7th, and receded below flood stage at 1:30pm CST on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred across the headwaters of the Little Sioux River on July 5 and resulted in a brief period of minor to locally moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 1 to 2 inches fell across Dickinson and northern Clay Counties on July 5, which resulted in minor flooding of the Little Sioux River near Spencer/Highway 18 (gage site SPII4) and a crest at 0.81 feet above flood stage on July 13. Some agricultural and pasture land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours. With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches from 4 to 6 inches in diameter were downed by thunderstorm winds on the Northwestern College campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred across the headwaters of the Little Sioux River on July 5 and resulted in a brief period of minor to locally moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches across Dickinson County on July 5 brought a rapid rise to the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4), with a crest into moderate flooding at 2.93 feet above flood stage on July 12. A significant amount of rural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred across the headwaters of the Little Sioux River on July 5 and resulted in a brief period of minor to locally moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 1 to 2 inches fell across Dickinson County on July 5, which resulted in minor flooding of the Little Sioux River near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) and a crest at 0.92 feet above flood stage on July 11. A significant amount of rural land was flooded, and water encroached on 170th Avenue north of Iowa Highway 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow and nearly stationary band of thunderstorms developed across Dixon and northern Dakota Counties during the late afternoon hours. Rainfall estimates of 3 to 5 inches occurred in a very small area from central Dixon to far northwestern Woodbury Counties, with the largest observed rainfall of 5.50 inches 4 miles west of McCook Lake, 2.77 inches at mesonet site AV951 in northern Sioux City, IA, 2.07 inches at river gage SXCI4 along Perry Creek in northern Sioux City, IA 2.01 inches in Concord, NE and 1.80 inches 3 miles southwest of Jefferson, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cars were stalled in deep standing water along west 19th Street in Sioux City. Several other low-lying roads were covered by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures have led to the expansion of severe drought conditions in portions of northwest and north central Iowa by late June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Palo Alto county began to experience severe drought conditions by late June as below normal rainfall persisted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought much needed rainfall to eastern Iowa the morning of November 4th through the 5th. This also brought two rounds of severe weather to eastern Iowa. The morning of November 4th, a few storms cells intensified quickly and produced sporadic reports of hail that was penny to ping pong ball sized. Heavy rain also fell with this storm system with 48 hour rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches across eastern Iowa with the heaviest rains being reported along a Ottumwa to Dubuque line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public relayed a report of 64 MPH recorded on a Davis Weather Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought much needed rainfall to eastern Iowa the morning of November 4th through the 5th. This also brought two rounds of severe weather to eastern Iowa. During the morning hours of the 5th, a cold front moved eastward across the area couple with a low level jet led to a rapidly strengthening line of thunderstorms across far eastern Iowa that produced strong winds. The strongest winds along this line occurred in Scott and Muscatine County where damaging winds were reported. Heavy rain also fell with this storm system with 48 hour rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches across eastern Iowa with the heaviest rains being reported along a Ottumwa to Dubuque line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Muscatine Airport measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 51 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms repeatedly moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of July 5th. Radar estimates suggest between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell from these storms which resulted in some localized flash flooding. Rocks and other debris were pushed onto some roads near McGregor (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused some flash flooding to occur near McGregor. Rocks and debris were pushed onto some roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms with heavy rain moved across parts of northeast Iowa during the morning of July 15th. Radar estimates indicate a band of 3 to 5 inches of rain fell across the northeast corner of Clayton County. This heavy rain pushed Bloody Run Creek out of its banks which took out a bridge in Bloody Run County Park west of Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused some flash flooding to occur across northeast Clayton County. Flood waters from Bloody Run Creek took out a bridge in the Bloody Run County Park west of Marquette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of July 23rd. These storms produced an EF1 tornado west of Waukon (Allamakee County) that damaged a farm and trees. Strong thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage to several buildings at the community college in Calmar (Winneshiek County) and flipped over a couple vehicles. The public school athletic fields in Calmar were also heavily damaged. The press box was blown off the high school football stands, metal light poles were downed at the softball field, and metal bleachers were thrown over a fence and onto the field. In Waukon, the roofs were damaged on numerous buildings and several trees and power lines were blown down. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Allamakee, Clayton and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred west of Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were common to start the month across much of central and southern Iowa.  However, a substantial rain event occurred on the 4th and 5th of the month helping to alleviate the drought in many locations by the Drought Monitor issuance on the 8th of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beneficial rainfall early in November help to alleviate severe drought conditions in Union county by the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down and roof damage with strong winds. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low for a few days July 7th and 8th. While the parameter space was not conducive to strong convection, the steep lapse rates, low LCLs, and sufficient 0-3 km CAPE resulted in a favorable environment for funnel cloud development both days. Several funnel clouds were reported across central and northern Iowa, along with two tornadoes documented under developing storms during the afternoon of July 7th.  These spin-ups remained in open areas with little damage. Storms were also efficient rain producers, with a pocket of 3 to 5 inches in Hardin and Grundy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was captured on a photograph and found on social media.  It developed underneath a developing shower and remained in rural cropland.  There were no damage indicators impacted, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis passed over the state and thunderstorms that had formed along a boundary in the early morning hours strengthened by midday. Despite relatively weak shear profiles, storms were able to organize enough to produce damaging wind gusts in Guthrie county. The storm complex continued into southeast Iowa into the afternoon, remaining below severe limits.||Rainfall in the Des Moines River basin was able to push the river, at Fleur Drive, briefly into flood stage on the 7th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms from the 14th into the 15th resulted in severe weather and heavy rainfall. A number of stations and reports of 4 inches or greater rainfall were received in and around Story County. As a result, the South Skunk River in Ames at US 30 briefly went into flood stage on the 15th of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Skunk River in Ames at US 30 went into flood stage, 21.5 feet, at 10am CST on June 15th, crested at 22.1 feet at 2:15 pm CST, and receded below flood stage at 9pm CST June 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa during the evening hours of July 23rd  and into northwest Illinois during the early morning of Sunday July 24th. The Dubuque County Airport measured a 67 MPH wind gust as storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust by the AWOS at Independence Airport (IIB).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho swept across South Dakota and into Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This resulted in a swath of  wind gust from 50 to 65 plus mph resulting in a number of power outages, tree damage, and a few damaged structures. A brief tornado occurred near Estherville with minor damage. As the bowing thunderstorm complex reach central Iowa the gusts had dropped below severe criteria given the less favorable environment with decreased deep layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management also reports downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low for a few days July 7th and 8th. While the parameter space was not conducive to strong convection, the steep lapse rates, low LCLs, and sufficient 0-3 km CAPE resulted in a favorable environment for funnel cloud development both days. Several funnel clouds were reported across central and northern Iowa, along with two tornadoes documented under developing storms during the afternoon of July 7th.  These spin-ups remained in open areas with little damage. Storms were also efficient rain producers, with a pocket of 3 to 5 inches in Hardin and Grundy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report, time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked east across Iowa Tuesday afternoon and evening bringing damaging wind, occasional lightning, and heavy rain to eastern IA and northwest IL.  The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. Another brief tornado occurred just west of Winthrop IA in an open field.  Since no visible damage was observed with either tornado, they were assigned an EF-U rating.  The NWS would like to thank Buchanan County Emergency Management for assistance with the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager for Benton County relayed a report of trees down at Wildcat Bluff State Park. This was likely caused by thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low for a few days July 7th and 8th. While the parameter space was not conducive to strong convection, the steep lapse rates, low LCLs, and sufficient 0-3 km CAPE resulted in a favorable environment for funnel cloud development both days. Several funnel clouds were reported across central and northern Iowa, along with two tornadoes documented under developing storms during the afternoon of July 7th.  These spin-ups remained in open areas with little damage. Storms were also efficient rain producers, with a pocket of 3 to 5 inches in Hardin and Grundy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was captured via a photograph and found on social media.  It was a brief landspout located south of Templeton Iowa impacting no damage indicator, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 3.06 inches.

A few non sever thunderstorms developed along the front, one of which produced a brief funnel cloud in northeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked east across Iowa Tuesday afternoon and evening bringing damaging wind, occasional lightning, and heavy rain to eastern IA and northwest IL.  The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. Another brief tornado occurred just west of Winthrop IA in an open field.  Since no visible damage was observed with either tornado, they were assigned an EF-U rating.  The NWS would like to thank Buchanan County Emergency Management for assistance with the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported large tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa during the evening hours of July 23rd  and into northwest Illinois during the early morning of Sunday July 24th. Storms produced mainly damaging winds along their path, with numerous reports of structure, tree, and power line damage from Buchanan County Iowa east into Jo Daviess County Illinois. The Dubuque County Airport measured a 67 MPH wind gust as storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter sent pictures via social media that shows a few branches near 6 inches in diameter down along with the trunk of a snapped tree. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of July 23rd. These storms produced an EF1 tornado west of Waukon (Allamakee County) that damaged a farm and trees. Strong thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage to several buildings at the community college in Calmar (Winneshiek County) and flipped over a couple vehicles. The public school athletic fields in Calmar were also heavily damaged. The press box was blown off the high school football stands, metal light poles were downed at the softball field, and metal bleachers were thrown over a fence and onto the field. In Waukon, the roofs were damaged on numerous buildings and several trees and power lines were blown down. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Allamakee, Clayton and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Decorah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary across Nebraska and into western Iowa in the pre-dawn early morning hours of July 11th. The storms had well established cold pools as they moved into Iowa and an area of favorable MUCAPE which helped to sustain the convection. Storms produced over 70 mph in western Iowa, weakening some into central Iowa with sub-severe wind gusts by the time the storms reached eastern Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved across Iowa in early June, this one resulting in severe storms across southwest Iowa. Modest CAPE around 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear that helped to organize storms and support rotating updrafts capable of large hail and damaging winds. By evening storms across southwest Iowa were producing up to golf ball sized hail and winds damaged several trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, sheds, and power lines reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a few hail stones up to half dollar sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of July 10th. Some of these storms produced hail, with the largest reported being quarter sized in Riceville (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Riceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough dropped out of the Canadian Rockies and across the upper midwest. Storms developed along a surface boundary in the afternoon, resulting in a few hail reports and a strong wind gust. In addition to the moderate CAPE , the backed wind profile supported some rotation within storms. While no tornadoes occurred there was a funnel cloud reported north of the Des Moines metro.  Later that night the deep warm cloud layer and high precipitable water values made for efficient rain producers. As storms continued overnight along a stationary boundary locally heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of funnel clouds received between Luther and Madrid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa during the evening hours of July 23rd  and into northwest Illinois during the early morning of Sunday July 24th. Storms produced mainly damaging winds along their path, with numerous reports of structure, tree, and power line damage from Buchanan County Iowa east into Jo Daviess County Illinois. The Dubuque County Airport measured a 67 MPH wind gust as storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported downed power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dubuque Airport ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 58 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough dropped out of the Canadian Rockies and across the upper midwest. Storms developed along a surface boundary in the afternoon, resulting in a few hail reports and a strong wind gust. In addition to the moderate CAPE , the backed wind profile supported some rotation within storms. While no tornadoes occurred there was a funnel cloud reported north of the Des Moines metro.  Later that night the deep warm cloud layer and high precipitable water values made for efficient rain producers. As storms continued overnight along a stationary boundary locally heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guthrie county emergency manager reported multiple roadways with running water over them. Not reported as widespread at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho swept across South Dakota and into Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This resulted in a swath of  wind gust from 50 to 65 plus mph resulting in a number of power outages, tree damage, and a few damaged structures. A brief tornado occurred near Estherville with minor damage. As the bowing thunderstorm complex reach central Iowa the gusts had dropped below severe criteria given the less favorable environment with decreased deep layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was reported by local law enforcement with portions of it also captured on video.  Some of the path was also evident in high resolution satellite imagery.  This tornado remained mostly in open cropland doing damage to corn and beans.  However, it did pass by a couple of farmsteads with EF0 damage to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds snapped a 150 foot tree in Sutherland - at the time the largest tree in O'Brien County.  Limbs which fell on powerlines resulted in up to 5 hours without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nine hour rain total of 4.60 inches.

Modest CAPE around 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear that helped to organize storms and support rotating updrafts capable of large hail and damaging winds. By evening storms across southwest Iowa were producing up to golf ball sized hail and winds damaged several trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off a machine shed. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts brought down numerous large tree limbs, with several trees snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds in George.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of MCVs resulted in severe storms in Iowa on the morning of June 13th. The first moved in from Nebraska and interacted with highly unstable airmass with MLCAPE analyzed at over 3000 J/kg in an area of strong warm air advection. This allowed storms to strengthen and produce nickel sized hail in the early morning. At the same time an MCV was moving into Minnesota with trailing convection into northern Iowa. By mid morning this had also collided with the unstable air with several reports of hail between nickel to golf ball sized in northern Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 3.99 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough produced widespread showers and thunderstorms across the Midwest. Meanwhile, isolated storms developed behind the primary area of convection while instability remained high and shear was still in place to help organize storms. Most of these remained sub severe, however one storm just south of Des Moines produced up to golf ball sized hail. Later that evening a cold front swept across the area. A few non sever thunderstorms developed along the front, one of which produced a brief funnel cloud in northeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought much needed rainfall to eastern Iowa the morning of November 4th through the 5th. This also brought two rounds of severe weather to eastern Iowa. The morning of November 4th, a few storms cells intensified quickly and produced sporadic reports of hail that was penny to ping pong ball sized. Heavy rain also fell with this storm system with 48 hour rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches across eastern Iowa with the heaviest rains being reported along a Ottumwa to Dubuque line.

A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in neighborhood with power outage. Delayed report, time estimated via radar.

Scattered storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of July 4th. A few of these storms became strong enough to produce some hail across Fayette and Clayton Counties. The largest reported hail was quarter sized in Littleport (Clayton County) and Elkport (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Littleport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were common to start the month across much of central and southern Iowa.  However, a substantial rain event occurred on the 4th and 5th of the month helping to alleviate the drought in many locations by the Drought Monitor issuance on the 8th of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beneficial rainfall early in November help to alleviate severe drought conditions in Jasper county by the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches down on farmstead. Report via social media, time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis passed over the state and thunderstorms that had formed along a boundary in the early morning hours strengthened by midday. Despite relatively weak shear profiles, storms were able to organize enough to produce damaging wind gusts in Guthrie county. The storm complex continued into southeast Iowa into the afternoon, remaining below severe limits.||Rainfall in the Des Moines River basin was able to push the river, at Fleur Drive, briefly into flood stage on the 7th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three outbuildings damaged and one destroyed. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked east across Iowa Tuesday afternoon and evening bringing damaging wind, occasional lightning, and heavy rain to eastern IA and northwest IL.  The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. Another brief tornado occurred just west of Winthrop IA in an open field.  Since no visible damage was observed with either tornado, they were assigned an EF-U rating.  The NWS would like to thank Buchanan County Emergency Management for assistance with the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa, allowing for a longer duration rainfall event  with central and southeast Iowa picking up a widespread two to three inches with a few location seeing up to four inches of rain. While storms were initially discrete with a few stronger cores, they remained below severe limits and quickly grew into a stratiform regime with little lightning. While this rain helped to ease drought conditions in parts of the state, the precipitation deficit for the year remained high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain total of 2.41 inches with rain still falling at time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air outbreak resulted in low temperatures between -20�� and -10�� December 22 and 23. On Thursday Dec 23, most ground stations in the area did not observe a high temperature above 0��. Strong wind gusts were observed all 3 days with the strongest gusts occurring on Dec 23. Peak wind gusts were between 30-50 mph. The coldest wind chills observed were between -51�� and -30��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interpolation of ground stations near the area had Harrison County mostly below -30�� wind chills for approximately 33 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size. By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.

With the approach of a strong mid-level wave, a cluster of multicellular storms evolved within a weakly unstable and weakly-sheared environment.  A fair amount of dry air at lower levels made for 600 J/kg or more of DCAPE, with storms producing some spotty significant wind damage along a track from near Paullina to around Primghar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large 100-year-old tree, and removed gutters from a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall continued across much of Iowa in September with expanding drought conditions over much of the state.  Severe drought conditions persisted in northwest and north central Iowa with some expansion of Extreme drought late in the month.  Severe drought also persisted in portions of central and southern Iowa with a brief period of extreme drought in the southeast during the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palo Alto county remained in severe drought for all of September with degredation to Extreme drought in portions of the county late in the month. Abnormally low rainfall continues to produce widespread drought conditions late into the growing season.  Impacts are likely to crop yields across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough supplied enough moisture and weak lift to sustain non sever convection through much of the morning of September 17th. By evening a secondary lobe of low pressure lifted into Iowa, enhancing convective development. MLCAPE over 2000 J/kg, curve hodographs, and steep mid level lapse rates supported large hail development. Convection lasted much of the evening and overnight across southern Iowa with several of the multicell storms producing severe hail. In addition, storms produced pockets of heavy rain with area receiving three to five inches of rain.

Abnormally low rainfall continues to produce widespread drought conditions late into the growing season. Impacts are likely to crop yields across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall continued across much of Iowa in September with expanding drought conditions over much of the state.  Severe drought conditions persisted in northwest and north central Iowa with some expansion of Extreme drought late in the month.  Severe drought also persisted in portions of central and southern Iowa with a brief period of extreme drought in the southeast during the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davis county remained in severe drought for all of September with degredation to extreme drought in portions of the county by the middle of the month.  This degredation was relatively short lived with some rainfall but severe conditions remained through the end of the month. Abnormally low rainfall continues to produce widespread drought conditions late into the growing season. Impacts are likely to crop yields across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough supplied enough moisture and weak lift to sustain non sever convection through much of the morning of September 17th. By evening a secondary lobe of low pressure lifted into Iowa, enhancing convective development. MLCAPE over 2000 J/kg, curve hodographs, and steep mid level lapse rates supported large hail development. Convection lasted much of the evening and overnight across southern Iowa with several of the multicell storms producing severe hail. In addition, storms produced pockets of heavy rain with area receiving three to five inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa, allowing for a longer duration rainfall event  with central and southeast Iowa picking up a widespread two to three inches with a few location seeing up to four inches of rain. While storms were initially discrete with a few stronger cores, they remained below severe limits and quickly grew into a stratiform regime with little lightning. While this rain helped to ease drought conditions in parts of the state, the precipitation deficit for the year remained high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 2.99 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size. By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the approach of a strong mid-level wave, a cluster of multicellular storms evolved within a weakly unstable and weakly-sheared environment.  A fair amount of dry air at lower levels made for 600 J/kg or more of DCAPE, with storms producing some spotty significant wind damage along a track from near Paullina to around Primghar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was removed from part of a farm outbuilding by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air outbreak resulted in low temperatures between -20�� and -10�� December 22 and 23. On Thursday Dec 23, most ground stations in the area did not observe a high temperature above 0��. Strong wind gusts were observed all 3 days with the strongest gusts occurring on Dec 23. Peak wind gusts were between 30-50 mph. The coldest wind chills observed were between -51�� and -30��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures at the Shenandoah AWOS remained mostly below -30�� for 23 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on the evening of September 18, 2022. Storms initially developed near the Iowa/Missouri border, with one storm near Bloomfield IA producing a well defined wall cloud before weakening. Storms developed and increased in strength just east of the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros as they slowly tracked east around the Interstate 80 corridor. Several large hail reports were received around Durant to south of Tipton, with the largest report being 2.5 inches. Storms also produced small hail and wind damage in the Quad Cities before spreading southeast into Henry County IL. Additional storms developed along the Iowa/Missouri/Illinois tri-state area, with several tornado warnings issued. Damage was reported in southern Hancock County near Tioga, and continued south into Adams County, although no tornadoes were confirmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch branches were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough supplied enough moisture and weak lift to sustain non sever convection through much of the morning of September 17th. By evening a secondary lobe of low pressure lifted into Iowa, enhancing convective development. MLCAPE over 2000 J/kg, curve hodographs, and steep mid level lapse rates supported large hail development. Convection lasted much of the evening and overnight across southern Iowa with several of the multicell storms producing severe hail. In addition, storms produced pockets of heavy rain with area receiving three to five inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail just shy of quarter sized. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface low lifted out of the desert southwest and into Iowa late on the 8th, with precipitation initially falling in the form of rain. As the system lifted into northern Iowa rain transitioned to snow after midnight with quick accumulations by sunrise of eight to ten inches. A few locations even reported a foot of snow. Further south accumulations were limited due to warmer temperatures, resulting in a sloppy wet snow and lesser accumulations of three inches or less.
