On March 5, 2022 a total of 15 tornadoes occurred across the state of Iowa as a warm front lifted across the area. Supercells initially developed across southwest Iowa in the vicinity of the triple point of the surface low with strong, rotating updrafts. Longevity was initially lacking with the storms, producing funnel clouds, brief tornadoes, and hail in southwest Iowa. As the cells moved northeast and matured, larger hail began to fall with golf ball sized hail falling in Adams county and baseball sized hail into Adair county. Following this period of large hail the storms produced a tornado which would remain on the ground for just over 70 miles, carving a path across central Iowa from southwest of Winterset to just northeast of Newton. The tornado peaked in Winterset, Iowa, producing EF-4 damage with wind speeds estimated near 170 mph. The tornado continued across the southern portion of the Des Moines metro area and to Newton. This is the furthest north EF-4 tornado in the CONUS this early in the year on record, the first EF-4 tornado in Iowa since October 4, 2013 which occurred in Woodbury and Cherokee Counties, and is the second longest tornado in Iowa since 1980, behind the longest occurring on June 7, 1984 at a length of 117 miles across southern Iowa. As the storm skimmed the southern Des Moines metro, cells across south central became tornadic, producing several destructive tornadoes including an EF-3 tornado in Chariton, Iowa. This heartbreaking day resulted in seven fatalities, six in Winterset and one in Chariton, making it the deadliest tornado outbreak in Iowa since 2008. At 15 tornadoes, this is also one of the earliest outbreaks of this magnitude to occur so early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was the next in a series of tornadoes from the same supercell which tracked across central Iowa on this day.  It formed southwest of the intersection of 370th Street and H Avenue and moved northeast.  While remaining mostly over cropland and wooded areas, the tornado did impact a couple different residences with EF1 damage to outbuildings along Highway 63 south of Tama and then EF2 damage to a house on Highway E64 southeast of Tama where the roof was removed.  Several cars were also tossed around at this location.   The tornado then moved into the Iowa River valley producing tree damage with the tornado dissipating just shy of Highway 30 near P Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Winter Storm Warning was issued for Dec 21 until the 23rd. Initially, peak snowfall amounts were forecast to range from 3 to 7 inches. As the event neared, the final forecast called for 2 to 3 inches across the area. The WSW was maintained as 40 to 50 mph wind gusts were expected to lead to near blizzard conditions. A brief period of freezing drizzle was also forecast and was observed.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2.5 inches with a peak of 2.5 inches observed in Fremont County. Snowfall amounts were difficult to measure accuately. Blizzard like conditions largely weren't realized. Some ground stations in southwest Iowa did observe visibility less than or equal to a mile for 2 to 3 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported 2.4 inches of snowfall. The AWOS at Council Bluffs reported visibility less than or equal to a mile for 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.

Very strong deep-layer shear developed as a result of an increase in the low-level jet and turning mid-level winds ahead of the approaching strong upper-level trough. A strong push of mid-level instability created up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, despite an environment with a strong sub-cloud dry layer. Storms moved rapidly northeast, with a cluster of hail producing storms in east central South Dakota. A second round of wind-producing storms erupted on the nose of the low-level jet across far northeast Nebraska, and translated quickly through southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-inch diameter tree branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up near the intersection of Fig Avenue and 140th Street.  On a northeastward track, the tornado impacted two farmsteads around a mile apart, with significant roof damage done to outbuildings at each location, as well as destruction of a  large grain bin.  The tornado tracked a few hundred yards beyond the final farm before dissipating in an open field. The estimated maximum wind was 90 mph and the average path width was 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station 3 miles north-northeast of Larchwood measured a wind gust of 87 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds.||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

A cluster of supercell thunderstorms moved through the region beginning on the evening of June 14 and continuing into the early morning hours of June 15. The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 75 to 115 mph, hail 1 to 3 inches in diameter, and two EF1 tornadoes. Minor flooding was also reported in Omaha. The storms caused widespread tree damage, and some structural damage to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent tornado began approximately 3 S Treynor. Ended 3 SE of Treynor. Tornado travelled mainly across crop and pastureland. Struck winery, destroying event center. Peripheral damage to trees and vines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind increased to 25 to 35 mph, with numerous gusts to 45 to 55 mph across northwest Iowa following an overnight frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured by the automated surface observing system site KSPW 3 miles west northwest of Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind increased to 25 to 35 mph, with numerous gusts to 45 to 55 mph across northwest Iowa following an overnight frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 50 mph was measured at State of Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSLI4 2 miles east-northeast of Truesdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a compact jet streak ejected out of a deep western U.S. trough, a strong area of low pressure developed and tracked northeast from Kansas to Iowa. Initial thermal profiles were warm enough to result in a brief period of light freezing rain before a transition to snow, but with generally light ice accumulation and little snow accumulation across northwest Iowa. A few rumbles of thunder were accompanied by small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain in the afternoon resulted in up to one-tenth of an inch ice accumulation at Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts  between 1 to 4 inches.  Some higher totals near 6 inches were reported in eastern Linn and Cedar counties.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas.  Many accidents were reported on Interstate 80, causing frequent backups.  Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area.  Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer 1 mile south of Salem reported 1.5 inches of snow.  Winds quickly increased with gusts of 40 to 50 MPH which led to blowing and drifting snow across the area. This led to blowing and drifting snow across the area. A peak gust of 41 MPH was reported at the Mount Pleasant Airport.. Additionally, this also produced wind chills of -20 to -30. The coldest wind chills occurred on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -43 near Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 2.5 inches near Monona and Postville. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts.

Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 45 mph created blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions occurring from the late morning into the afternoon of the 23rd. The highest record gust was 45 mph at a mesonet site in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter system impacted Iowa and much of the Midwest in the days leading up to Christmas 2022. Snow began to fall across Iowa on the 21st and lasted through the morning of the 22nd, blanketing most of the state in a widespread depth of two to four inches. At the same time, temperature began to plummet with wind chills across the area dropping into the minus 30s and minus 40s by the morning of the 22nd. As the system began to shift east the tight pressure gradient and strong cold air advection allowed winds to increase, gusting to over 40 mph in many locations by the 23rd. A few places even recorded wind gusts of 50 to 58 mph. The strong winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions across portions of central and all of northern Iowa on the 23rd with visibility near zero. Winds gradually eased overnight with significant improvement to visibility by Christmas Eve morning. Conditions significantly impacted holiday travel with dozens of car accidents reported and a closure of interstate 35. This was a historic event. While extreme cold occurs with regularity in Iowa, the duration of this arctic outbreak set this event apart. The combination with blizzard conditions resulted in especially dangerous conditions putting anyone stranded at severe risk.

At the same time, temperature began to plummet with wind chills across the area dropping into the minus 30s and minus 40s by the morning of the 22nd. As the system began to shift east the tight pressure gradient and strong cold air advection allowed winds to increase, gusting to over 40 mph in many locations by the 23rd. A few places even recorded wind gusts of 50 to 58 mph. The strong winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions across portions of central and all of northern Iowa on the 23rd with visibility near zero. Winds gradually eased overnight with significant improvement to visibility by Christmas Eve morning. Conditions significantly impacted holiday travel with dozens of car accidents reported and a closure of interstate 35. This was a historic event. While extreme cold occurs with regularity in Iowa, the duration of this arctic outbreak set this event apart. The combination with blizzard conditions resulted in especially dangerous conditions putting anyone stranded at severe risk.
Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with frozen ground conditions, despite up to double to triple normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought persisted through December. Monthly precipitation at Sioux Rapids and Storm Lake was around 200 percent of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -43 near Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across Clayton County. The highest reported total was 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 in Guttenberg. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 3 inches of snow fell across Fayette County. The highest reported total was 3.8 inches in Fayette. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 45 mph created blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions occurring from the late morning into the afternoon of the 23rd. The highest record gust was 47 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -48F near Storm Lake (AWOS site KSLB) and 2 miles northeast of Truesdale (Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSLI4), and -47 1 mile south of Newell (Iowa mesonet site NWLI4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across Floyd County. The highest reported total was 3 inches near Nashua. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 45 mph created blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions occurring from the late morning into the afternoon of the 23rd. The highest record gust was 49 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport. County snow plows were pulled off the roads on the 23rd and emergency management recommended no travel in the county.

A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts  between 1 to 4 inches.  Some higher totals near 6 inches were reported in eastern Linn and Cedar counties.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas.  Many accidents were reported on Interstate 80, causing frequent backups.  Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area.  Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freshly fallen snow, combined with strong northwest winds sustained between 20 to 30 knots and gusts up to 37 knots during the afternoon produced blizzard conditions across the county.  Numerous spotter reports, web and snowplow cameras showed blizzard conditions outside of cities and towns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer. With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of northwest Iowa. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to around one-quarter inch on elevated and road surfaces at Melvin. Branches up to 6 inches in diameter were taken down by ice accumulation.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 6.3 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 1.2 miles north-northeast of Larchwood and 3.7 inches at the COOP site in Rock Rapids. Other public reports across the county ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts  between 1 to 4 inches.  Some higher totals near 6 inches were reported in eastern Linn and Cedar counties.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas.  Many accidents were reported on Interstate 80, causing frequent backups.  Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area.  Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Clinton reported 3.0 inches of snow. A trained spotter northeast of Clinton reported 1.9 inches of snow. Winds increased on the 22nd with gusts over 40 to 50 MPH which produced blizzard conditions especially in rural and open areas for several hours. The wind chill values at the Clinton Airport was as cold as -30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -53F 7 miles southwest of Ocheyedan (Iowa State mesonet site SBEI4) and -47F 2 miles north-northeast of Sibley (Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSYI4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer. With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of northwest Iowa. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to around one-quarter inch on elevated and road surfaces at Cherokee.  Multiple branches up to 6 inches in diameter were taken down by ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -43 near Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of arctic air and wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph created wind chills of 20 to 40 below zero across Chickasaw County. The lowest calculated wind chill was 41 below at a mesonet site in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter system impacted Iowa and much of the Midwest in the days leading up to Christmas 2022. Snow began to fall across Iowa on the 21st and lasted through the morning of the 22nd, blanketing most of the state in a widespread depth of two to four inches. At the same time, temperature began to plummet with wind chills across the area dropping into the minus 30s and minus 40s by the morning of the 22nd. As the system began to shift east the tight pressure gradient and strong cold air advection allowed winds to increase, gusting to over 40 mph in many locations by the 23rd. A few places even recorded wind gusts of 50 to 58 mph. The strong winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions across portions of central and all of northern Iowa on the 23rd with visibility near zero. Winds gradually eased overnight with significant improvement to visibility by Christmas Eve morning. Conditions significantly impacted holiday travel with dozens of car accidents reported and a closure of interstate 35. This was a historic event. While extreme cold occurs with regularity in Iowa, the duration of this arctic outbreak set this event apart. The combination with blizzard conditions resulted in especially dangerous conditions putting anyone stranded at severe risk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common. Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on December 15, with strong and gusty winds developing shortly after this time. Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile occurred from late morning through the afternoon, with a peak wind gust reaching 39 mph at the Iowa State Road Weather Information System site DONI4 near Doon. Snowfall totaled 3.3 inches at COOP site RKRI4 in Rock Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 2 to 4 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of northwest Iowa on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended.  Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media.  Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills. Winds resulted in spotty power outages around the Iowa Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 52 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KSLB near Storm Lake and 48 mph at Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSLI4 near Truesdale, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fall reports ranged from 2.5 to 3.5 inches across the county. Peak wind gusts ranged from 40 to 50 MPH with a peak wind gust at the Manchester Airport of 49 MPH. This led to widespread blowing and drifting snow across the area resulting in blizzard conditions across the area. These winds also produced a wind chill of -23 in Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common. Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on December 15, with strong and gusty winds developing shortly after this time. Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile occurred from late morning through early afternoon, with a peak wind gust reaching 41 mph at the Iowa State Road Weather Information System site RTNI4 near Orange City. Snowfall totaled 3.7 inches at COOP site ORCI4 in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 2 to 4 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of northwest Iowa on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended.  Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media.  Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills. Winds resulted in spotty power outages around the Iowa Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 40 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KCKP at Cherokee, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -43 near Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of arctic air and wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph created wind chills of 20 to 40 below zero across Winneshiek County. The lowest calculated wind chill was 41 below at a mesonet site near Jackson Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter system impacted Iowa and much of the Midwest in the days leading up to Christmas 2022. Snow began to fall across Iowa on the 21st and lasted through the morning of the 22nd, blanketing most of the state in a widespread depth of two to four inches. At the same time, temperature began to plummet with wind chills across the area dropping into the minus 30s and minus 40s by the morning of the 22nd. As the system began to shift east the tight pressure gradient and strong cold air advection allowed winds to increase, gusting to over 40 mph in many locations by the 23rd. A few places even recorded wind gusts of 50 to 58 mph. The strong winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions across portions of central and all of northern Iowa on the 23rd with visibility near zero. Winds gradually eased overnight with significant improvement to visibility by Christmas Eve morning. Conditions significantly impacted holiday travel with dozens of car accidents reported and a closure of interstate 35. This was a historic event. While extreme cold occurs with regularity in Iowa, the duration of this arctic outbreak set this event apart. The combination with blizzard conditions resulted in especially dangerous conditions putting anyone stranded at severe risk.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 4.4 inches at the COOP site in Remsen and 4.0 inches at the COOP site in Le Mars.

At the same time, temperature began to plummet with wind chills across the area dropping into the minus 30s and minus 40s by the morning of the 22nd. As the system began to shift east the tight pressure gradient and strong cold air advection allowed winds to increase, gusting to over 40 mph in many locations by the 23rd. A few places even recorded wind gusts of 50 to 58 mph. The strong winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions across portions of central and all of northern Iowa on the 23rd with visibility near zero. Winds gradually eased overnight with significant improvement to visibility by Christmas Eve morning. Conditions significantly impacted holiday travel with dozens of car accidents reported and a closure of interstate 35. This was a historic event. While extreme cold occurs with regularity in Iowa, the duration of this arctic outbreak set this event apart. The combination with blizzard conditions resulted in especially dangerous conditions putting anyone stranded at severe risk.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 2.3 inches 5 miles west-northwest of Holstein and 1.2 inches 5 miles northeast of Battle Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts  between 1 to 4 inches.  Some higher totals near 6 inches were reported in eastern Linn and Cedar counties.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas.  Many accidents were reported on Interstate 80, causing frequent backups.  Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area.  Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 4.0 inches by a trained spotter in Lisbon to 5.0 inches by a trained spotter 2 miles west southwest of Marian. Winds increased with gusts up to 35 MPH leading to reduced visibilities from blowing and drifting snow. Temperatures also dropped quickly with cold winds chills below zero.

This led to reduced visibility and blowing and drifting snow. Additionally, this produced wind chills as cold as -26. The coldest wind chills occurred on the evening of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to northeast Iowa ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest totals around 5 inches at Lock and Dam 10 near Guttenberg (Clayton County) and 4 inches north of Frankville (Winneshiek County). Strong winds of 30 to 45 mph from the 23rd into the 24th created blowing and drifting snow. Whiteouts were common and blizzard conditions occurred across Floyd, Chickasaw and Fayette County. Numerous rural roads were drifted shut across these counties. State snow plows were pulled off the roads across all of the region for the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The wind combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -43 near Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of arctic air and wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph created wind chills of 20 to 40 below zero across Fayette County. The lowest calculated wind chill was 42 below at the Oelwein airport.

A strong winter storm brought widespread snow to the region Thursday, with amounts  between 1 to 4 inches.  Some higher totals near 6 inches were reported in eastern Linn and Cedar counties.  Strong winds also accompanied the snow, which caused significant blowing and drifting, especially in rural areas.  Many accidents were reported on Interstate 80, causing frequent backups.  Even stronger winds were observed on Friday across the area.  Wind gusts between 40-50 mph were common, creating ground blizzard conditions primarily along and north of Interstate 80.||Additionally, temperatures dropped sharply behind the arctic cold front on Thursday and continued to fall throughout the day.  Single digit temperatures with wind chills between -20 and -40 degrees were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from a public report of 1.5 inches 1 mile east southeast of Muscatine to 3.0 inches from a COOP observer 4 miles north northeast of Muscatine.. Winds increased quickly with gusts up to 45 MPH reported at the Muscatine Airport. This led to reduced visibility and blowing and drifting snow. Additionally, this produced wind chills as cold as -25. The coldest wind chills occurred on the evening of the 23rd.
